summary:
The Spanish Conquest of the Aztec Empire (1519–1521 AD) was one of the most dramatic and consequential events in world history: a force of approximately 500 Spanish soldiers under [[event:3624|Hernán Cortés]], allied with tens of thousands of indigenous peoples who resented Aztec domination, overthrew the [[event:3870|Aztec Empire]] and its capital [[event:2261|Tenochtitlan]] in two years, ending indigenous political sovereignty in central Mexico and establishing the Viceroyalty of New Spain. The conquest's speed was enabled by multiple compounding factors: European diseases (especially smallpox) devastated the population before and during the campaign; the Aztec political system of tributary domination had created numerous alienated subject peoples eager to ally with the Spanish against their overlords; and Cortés proved a brilliant manipulator of Aztec political dynamics, exploiting the factional tensions around Emperor Moctezuma II.

The campaign unfolded in dramatic phases. Cortés arrived on the Gulf Coast in 1519, made alliances with the Tlaxcalans (inveterate Aztec enemies), and marched to Tenochtitlan, where he was received by Moctezuma — possibly in fulfillment of prophecies associated with the feathered serpent god Quetzalcoatl. Cortés seized Moctezuma as a hostage but was driven from the city in the disastrous Noche Triste (June 1520); he regrouped, gathered more indigenous allies, and laid systematic siege to Tenochtitlan. The city's [[event:3625|fall in August 1521]] — after three months of house-by-house fighting and epidemic disease — ended the most powerful indigenous civilization in Mesoamerica. The conquest opened central Mexico to Spanish colonization and extraction: the silver mines of [[event:2425|Potosí]] and New Spain would fund the Spanish empire for a century. The demographic catastrophe of the conquest — through war, disease, and exploitation — reduced the indigenous population of central Mexico from an estimated 25 million in 1519 to under 2 million by 1600.
